file_name: SLOVAKWIRELESSFINANCECOBV_03_28_2001-EX-4.(B)(II).3-Maintenance and support contract for SICAP(R) modules.txt

perturbation.original_text: After the initial period of two years, the maintenance and support contract shall be automatically renewed for a period of one year on each renewal date, unless one of the parties terminates the maintenance and support contract through written notification to the other party in the form of a registered letter with proof of receipt, at least six (6) weeks prior to the renewal date.
perturbation.changed_text: After the initial period of two years, the maintenance and support contract shall be automatically renewed for a period of one year on each renewal date, unless one of the parties terminates the maintenance and support contract through written notification to the other party, at least two (2) weeks prior to the renewal date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from six weeks to two weeks could violate Slovakian law if it is deemed an unreasonably short period to allow for transition or finding alternative service providers. Slovak contract law may imply a requirement for reasonable notice, especially in ongoing service agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovak Civil Code (Act No. 40/1964 Coll.) relating to general principles of contract law and reasonable notice periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the sources are in Slovak, Slovak Civil Code outlines acceptable contract laws and reasonable terms for contract termination. Reducing the notice period from six weeks to two weeks might be deemed unreasonable or exploitative for smaller suppliers dealing with larger entities. The reduction in time does not allow a reasonable amount of time to seek alternatives or to properly prepare for the business change, thus breaching the Slovak Civil Code.

perturbation.justification: The change from six weeks to two weeks for termination notice is a potentially significant reduction that might contradict the Slovak Civil Code's implied requirement for reasonable notice, as explained. The Law Explanation says that this reduction "does not allow a reasonable amount of time to seek alternatives or to properly prepare for the business change, thus breaching the Slovak Civil Code.". Scraped Law Snippet 2 points to the existence of the Slovak Civil Code. The Perturbation explanation also points to this possibility. Given this reasoning, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: In case of a substantial breach of this maintenance and support contract by either party, which is not remedied within thirty days from the other party's notice thereof, the other party shall have the right to terminate this maintenance and support contract with immediate effect without juridical procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: In case of a substantial breach of this maintenance and support contract by either party, which is not remedied within ten days from the other party's notice thereof, the other party shall have the right to terminate this maintenance and support contract with immediate effect without juridical procedures.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 30 days to 10 days for a substantial breach might conflict with Slovakian legal standards for fairness and reasonable opportunity to remedy the breach. Slovak law may require a longer period depending on the nature of the breach and the complexity of the remedy. A shorter period could be deemed insufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovak Civil Code (Act No. 40/1964 Coll.) relating to general principles of contract law and reasonable time to remedy breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the sources are in Slovak, Slovak Civil Code outlines the remedy to breach of contract and a potential cure period that is reasonable and the reduction might violate this portion of the law. Reducing the time to remedy the breech from 30 days to 10 days is a reduction of 2/3s of the original timeframe which could prevent the offending party from successfully completing any required actions in order to maintain the contractual obligations.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period for a breach from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation suggests this reduction might violate Slovak Civil Code, which requires a reasonable cure period. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the Slovak Civil Code (40/1964 Zb. Občiansky zákonník), indicating this law is relevant. The Perturbation Explanation elaborates that Slovak law may require a longer period depending on the breach, and a shorter period could be deemed insufficient, creating an inconsistency Legal Contradiction. The change directly reduces the time to remedy a breach, which could be seen as preventing the party from successfully maintaining contractual obligations. This contradiction is based on potentially insufficient time for remedy under Slovak law.
